issues.title: Update time field in examples to match the nextUpdate definition
issues.author: highlunder
issues.body: nextUpdate is defined as a time delta, but the examples use absolute time. They need to be updated

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: Examples have been updated to use the delta time.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: Fixed

issues.title: Define the serialNumberHash + CertHash as truncated hashes
issues.author: highlunder
issues.body:
From the meeting: we don't want to spend 32 byte on serialNumberHash/CertHash, but rather use truncated versions.

[What format/standard should we refer to? Something similar to kid = first 8 bytes of SHA-256(pubkey)?]

comments.author: highlunder
comments.body: Related question: In my quick experiments, I have trouble reproducing the OCSP example identity SHA-256 hashes. Can you share the exact bytes for which the hashes are produced?

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: I think truncated hash is OK. But I am not sure truncating hash to 64 bits is still secure enough.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: I registered now two truncated hash algorithms, SHA256/160 and SM3/160 which take the first 20 bytes of the original output. Using shorter (e.g. 8 bytes) may have security problem (I am not sure). Since the collision rate to have two serial numbers (1-20 bytes) is too high (e.g. 2^-32 for 8 bytes hash value).

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body:
> Related question: In my quick experiments, I have trouble reproducing the OCSP example identity SHA-256 hashes. Can you share the exact bytes for which the hashes are produced?

The original serial numbers can be extracted from the X.509 OCSP Requests and Responses. E.g. for the examples in section `Simple OCSP Request Example`. The serial number can be extracted via the command `openssl asn1parse -i -in file.pem` with output
```
~% openssl asn1parse -i -in x509ocspreq.pem 
    0:d=0  hl=3 l= 149 cons: SEQUENCE          
    3:d=1  hl=3 l= 146 cons:  SEQUENCE          
    6:d=2  hl=2 l= 109 cons:   SEQUENCE          
    8:d=3  hl=2 l= 107 cons:    SEQUENCE          
   10:d=4  hl=2 l= 105 cons:     SEQUENCE          
   12:d=5  hl=2 l=  13 cons:      SEQUENCE          
   14:d=6  hl=2 l=   9 prim:       OBJECT            :sha256
   25:d=6  hl=2 l=   0 prim:       NULL              
   27:d=5  hl=2 l=  32 prim:      OCTET STRING      [HEX DUMP]:32538E7485BD37AE454F1F0D2B9755D758B91F6E8E3A7AF397A0F400605C6FF8
   61:d=5  hl=2 l=  32 prim:      OCTET STRING      [HEX DUMP]:CC11C8740A0509EC4DA14CE5886E72364C0F63B60ED6347F13C16EE725B766A2
   95:d=5  hl=2 l=  20 prim:      INTEGER           :1234123412341234123412341234123412341234
  117:d=2  hl=2 l=  33 cons:   cont [ 2 ]        
  119:d=3  hl=2 l=  31 cons:    SEQUENCE          
  121:d=4  hl=2 l=  29 cons:     SEQUENCE          
  123:d=5  hl=2 l=   9 prim:      OBJECT            :OCSP Nonce
  134:d=5  hl=2 l=  16 prim:      OCTET STRING      [HEX DUMP]:11111111111111111111111111111111
```

 And then you may verify the SHA-256 hash value via `echo -n "1234123412341234123412341234123412341234" | xxd -r -p | sha256sum`.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body:
For a simple (usually unsigned) OCSP request : we have 2 hash values (issuerCertHash, serialNumberHash), and
for a simple (always signed) OCSP response: we have 3 hash values (issuerCertHash, serialNumberHash, responderCertHash).

For the serialNumberHash, the hash (algorithm) must be cryptographically secure. But for the issuerCertHash and responderCertHash, we may use shorter hash value (e.g. 8 bytes), it is used to identify the corresponding certificate among small certificate pool.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: In the latest version, I changed *certHash from length of full length to HashId8 (8 bytes), and SerialNumberHash to HashId20 (20 bytes). See commit https://github.com/lijun-nio/idc509rev/commit/7695bd0e08dcd98489e65187eea97488610b1c91 and https://github.com/lijun-nio/idc509rev/commit/7dc2c83e2fd790a4ad4e8ce2eae849981950bc42 for details.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: Fixed.

issues.title: Update size reduction tables
issues.author: highlunder
issues.body: Pending other updates; after agreeing on the hash trunctation format, we need to update the size comparison

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: My current idea is to define dedicated integer value for the truncated hash algorithm, instead to introduce a new field to specify the hash value size. For the examples in appendix, the problem is there are no OIDs for SHA256/160 and SM3/160, so it is difficult to compare the reduction of C509 compared to X509. Another approach is to use SHA-1, but using a broken hash algorithm in the example is not suitable.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: We need to keep this issue open, since due to the future change the size comparison will also change.

comments.body: Fixed.

issues.title: Enable building and deployment of the HTML Editor's Copy
issues.author: marco-tiloca-sics
issues.body:
@lijun-nio , on the GitHub web interface for this repository, please:

* Go on "Settings" (on the top), then "Pages" on the left.

* In the main frame, under "Build and deployment":

  * Under "Source", select "Deploy from a branch"

  * Under "Branch", select the "gh-pages" branch and the "/ (root)" folder, then hit "Save".

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body:
This seems to be an enterprise feature.

I got this message after clicking "Pages" in the main frame:

"Upgrade or make this repository public to enable Pages"

comments.author: marco-tiloca-sics
comments.body:
Not surprising.

We can keep the issue open as a reminder. After we submit, we can make the repository public and this fix should become possible.

issues.title: Should SHA-1 really own the cheapest 1-byte code point (value 0)?
issues.author: highlunder
issues.body: Currently, SHA-1 owns the cheapest 1-byte code point, value 0. Shouldn't that spot be given to a non-broken alg. (-256)? And demote -1 to a high range value

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: I will assign a negative value for SHA-1. Another question is do we need SHA-1? We can replace it with truncated e.g. SHA256/160.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: Now SHA-1 has `-1`

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body: In the latest version, I removed SHA-1.

comments.author: highlunder
comments.body: Ok! Then I consider this issue ready to close :)

issues.title: Prepare for IETF 126 Vienna
issues.author: lijun-nio
issues.body:
@rikard-sics @marco-tiloca-sics @highlunder I do not have and plan known changes. Please review the latest I.D.

After that, @rikard-sics please upload the I.D. and reserve a presentation time slot in the COSE-WG.

comments.author: marco-tiloca-sics
comments.body: Please check the fixes to the CDDL notation at https://github.com/lijun-nio/idc509rev/commit/3f3bcf94797ea5da6eaff07a8f765c813010149f

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body:
> Please check the fixes to the CDDL notation at [3f3bcf9](https://github.com/lijun-nio/idc509rev/commit/3f3bcf94797ea5da6eaff07a8f765c813010149f)

What exactly?

comments.author: marco-tiloca-sics
comments.body:
Before the commit https://github.com/lijun-nio/idc509rev/commit/3f3bcf94797ea5da6eaff07a8f765c813010149f  :

* Figure 2 was still using "bytes" as CBOR type for "serialNumberHash", instead of the new "HashId20" (2 instances).

* Figure 3 was missing the definition of "HashId8" and "HashId20".

The commit fixes that.

comments.author: lijun-nio
comments.body:
I submitted the last changes before the IETF meeting.

 @rikard-sics please upload the I.D. and reserve a presentation time slot in the COSE-WG.

comments.author: rikard-sics
comments.body:
> @rikard-sics please upload the I.D. and reserve a presentation time slot in the COSE-WG.

Thanks! Yeah I had a look at the draft also and it looks good to me

I will go ahead and upload it before the cutoff and reserve a slot in COSE.

comments.author: rikard-sics
comments.body: I will also create slides and send out to you all for review.
